what_it_does: Acceptance test that verifies the capabilities() response includes seven node states, each carrying an additive render block with token, icon, and shape; that every render token references a real custom-property design token in the corpus CSS; and that existing capability fields remain undisturbed.
observations: The module is framed as a structural acceptance suite, explicitly excluding aesthetic judgement.
It parses design-system.css with a regex to collect real corpus design tokens and asserts each render.token is in that set.
If the corpus CSS is absent, the token cross-check is skipped and surfaced as a flagged note rather than failing silently.
It checks that label, means, applies_to, and other capability keys are still present, emphasizing additive change.
The docstring lists out-of-lane follow-ups: missing CSS classes for failed/live/empty and unregistered icon/shape vocabularies.
It uses a global PASS counter and manual check() helper instead of a test framework like pytest or unittest.
A temporary FsStore and NodeRegistry are set up to call Suite.capabilities().
standards: The registry-is-truth cross-check between backend render tokens and the corpus CSS is the strongest signal.
The explicit additive-preservation assertions for both node-state fields and top-level capability keys stand out.
conventions: Heavy docstring-first framing of intent, scope, and known follow-ups.
Manual assertion helper with a global pass counter.
sys.path insertion to import project modules from a relative test location.
Regex-based parsing of a sibling design-system.css file for ground-truth tokens.
concerns: Global mutable PASS state makes the test non-reentrant and harder to integrate with standard runners.
Token extraction regex r"(--[a-z0-9-]+)\s*:" may miss or mis-collect CSS custom properties depending on formatting.
The test depends on a hardcoded corpus CSS path that may not exist in all checkouts.
No use of pytest/unittest means reporting, fixtures, and CI integration are ad-hoc.
notes: A structural acceptance gate that tries to stop the frontend from hardcoding state visuals by forcing backend render tokens to map to real corpus CSS custom properties.
questions: What are the exact provisional icon and shape values assigned to each state?
How does Suite.capabilities() assemble the node_states list and where is the render block injected?
Is design-system.css guaranteed to be present in production checkouts or only in full-repo checkouts?
Why is this implemented as a standalone script rather than a pytest test case?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the implementation: it structurally validates the new render field and its corpus-token backing while leaving aesthetic grading outside scope.
apparent_themes: Separation of machine-gradable structure from human design critique.
Acceptance testing at the capabilities() boundary.
intention_signals: The file appears to be enforcing that visual state tokens are served by the backend and anchored to an existing corpus design-token registry, so the frontend does not invent or hardcode a state-to-token vocabulary.
novelty: It treats the frontend CSS design-system file as the authoritative registry for backend-served render tokens, creating a cross-layer structural validation.
contribution: Provides a structural acceptance check for node-state render metadata and a registry-is-truth cross-reference to the corpus design-token CSS, plus explicit additive-preservation coverage for capabilities().
summary_for_embedding: An acceptance test script that validates each of the seven node states served by capabilities() carries a render block whose token references a real design-system.css custom property, while preserving existing capability structure.
intention_for_embedding: To prevent hardcoded frontend state-to-visual mappings by gating backend-render tokens against the corpus CSS registry and ensuring the change is additive.

symbols.does: Manual assertion helper that increments a global pass counter when a condition holds.
symbols.description: check(label, cond) asserts the supplied condition and, on success, increments the module-level PASS counter and prints an ok message. It is the only assertion mechanism used in the script.

symbols.does: Parses the corpus design-system.css to return the set of defined custom-property names.
symbols.description: corpus_tokens() reads design/design-system.css relative to the project root and extracts all custom-property names matching the pattern --[a-z0-9-]+ via regex, returning them as a set. It returns None if the CSS file is absent, signaling that the token cross-check should be skipped.
